title: Soumak stitch in tapestry weaving
subtitle: A wrapped-warp technique that creates a textured, ribbed surface in tapestry
excerpt: Soumak is a wrapping technique used in tapestry weaving: the weft wraps around each warp thread (or groups of threads) rather than passing straight through the shed. The result is a textured, slightly raised surface with a characteristic diagonal or herringbone pattern. Soumak is used as an area-fill stitch or as a border element and does not require a separate plain weave row to secure it, though a plain weave row is often woven between soumak rows for stability.
sourceNotes: Soumak is a historical textile technique found in Caucasian and Middle Eastern rugs and weavings. The name and technique are documented in textile archaeology and weaving instruction.

content.text: around each warp thread forward over 2 warps, then back under 1 warp (the standard 2/1 soumak sequence). This creates a diagonal rib on the surface. Reversing the direction on alternate rows creates a herringbone pattern. Soumak does not interlace structurally without a plain weave ground, so most tapestry applications alternate soumak rows with plain weave rows.

items.name: Frame loom or upright tapestry loom, warped
Weft yarn in working colour (smooth, medium weight)
Blunt darning needle (for threading the weft)

content.text: Step 1: Thread the weft on the needle
Thread a length of weft yarn on a blunt darning needle. Start at the left selvedge. Leave a 5-cm tail at the back.

Pass the needle over the first two warp threads, then bring it back under the second thread only (going left). The weft now loops around the first warp and lies angled to the right. Move to the next warp thread and repeat: over 2 warps, back under 1. Work consistently across the full warp width.
Step 3: Insert a plain weave row between soumak rows

After each soumak row, beat it down with the tapestry comb and weave a plain weave pick through the shed. The plain weave pick locks the soumak row in place and prevents the piece from pulling out of shape.

Work the first soumak row left to right (over 2, back under 1 going left). Return right to left for the second soumak row (over 2, back under 1 going right). The two rows of opposing diagonals create a herringbone or V-shaped texture.

items.fix: Leave a small arc of weft yarn before tightening each wrap. The extra length allows the row to sit flat without pulling.
items.cause: Weft tension too tight: the wrapping pulls the warp inward.
items.symptom: Soumak rows pulling the warp threads together, narrowing the piece

items.fix: Count out loud as you work: over 2, back 1, over 2, back 1. A rhythmic count is the most reliable check.
items.cause: The over-2 / back-1 sequence was not maintained consistently.
items.symptom: Surface looks irregular with uneven rib spacing
